$schema_doc: JSON content schema for build_teardown.js. Every field is optional — sections without content are simply omitted from the document. **bold** markdown is supported inline within string values.
competitor_name: Required. Renders as the title-page header. e.g. 'SpotOn', 'Toast', 'Square'.
prepared_date: Required. Free-form date string for the title page footer. e.g. 'April 2026'.
executive_summary: Array of paragraph strings, OR a single string. Each becomes its own paragraph.
Use **bold** to emphasize key facts (dollar amounts, percentages, named entities, strategic conclusions).
Last paragraph should typically lead with **Why this matters for PAR:** to make the relevance explicit.

company_snapshot.$schema_doc: Object of label → value. Renders as a 2-column fact table. Order is preserved.
company_snapshot.Customer Base: Number of locations / customers (with source caveats)

strategy.thesis: 1–2 paragraphs articulating the company's core strategic bet. What do they believe about the market that competitors don't?

pillars.title: Pillar name (short)
pillars.body: 1-paragraph explanation of the pillar. Why does it matter? How does it differ from competitors? What does it cost?

inflection_points: Bullet list. Each entry should start with **Year + Month — Event title** then explain implication. Keep to 5–7 high-signal items only.

icp.intro: Short framing paragraph explaining how segments are organized and which are most threatening to PAR.

segments.title: Section heading. e.g. 'Primary ICP — The Sweet Spot' or 'Secondary ICP — Local Multi-Unit'
segments.note: Optional follow-up paragraph after the table — typically used for the 'aspirational ICP where they meet PAR' commentary.

anti_icp: Bullets describing segments the competitor explicitly does NOT target. Important for PAR sales — these are safe zones.

product_diff.overview: 1 paragraph framing how the competitor's product strategy compares to peers (Toast, Square, NCR Voyix, etc.).
products: Bullet list of named product modules. Format: **Product Name** — short description.
strengths: Bullets covering what they do better than typical competitors. Each should begin with a **bold capability name**: ...
weaknesses: Bullets covering structural product weaknesses, framed as PAR opportunities. Same **bold capability name**: ... pattern.
product_diff.pricing: 1 paragraph on pricing posture, contract terms, payments-attach model, ETF / negotiation patterns.

swot.intro: Optional italic framing line — e.g. 'PAR-relevant SWOT — emphasis on signals that should affect PAR's competitive posture.'
strengths: 6 bullet items, each one short clause

news_intro: 1 paragraph framing — when to use the dashboard's News & Developments tab vs. this curated narrative.

news.headline: 1-line news headline (you, the analyst, write this — it's a paraphrase, not a verbatim copy)
news.source: Publication name + paraphrased article title — e.g. 'TechCrunch — SpotOn growth round at $3.15B'
news.takeaway: 1–3 sentences: what this means for PAR specifically. This is the value-add over the auto-fetched feed.

engage: Bullets — segments / battlefields where PAR has structural advantage. **Bold the segment name**: explanation.
avoid: Bullets — segments where PAR should not engage directly with this competitor.
signals: Bullets — what to watch for in the Competitive Intelligence Monitor that would change the threat calculus.
actions: Bullets — 3–5 concrete things PAR teams should do as a result of this teardown.
